Audio: I'm sorry. ready to go finally. So this is our lecture 11. I'm actually very excited about this lecture. This is a lecture that we're going to actually get some practice, but everything a bit seen in a previous lecture. We're going to practice a lot more. So we are going to see a new model. So it's called a constant model. We're going to talk about Some more last functions we're going to try. Keep optimizing models and then see how this calculus thing that we started using in fitting a model is going to actually be really helpful and then we're going to also just briefly talk about transformation. You had seen transformation before in the visualization lecture, but you're going to again see it one more time and you're going to see how it can be really helpful in linear regression. So this is where we are. So think about this lecture as a like kind of a debrief lecture plus a little bit of examples that we're going to go over so that it connects us from the previous lecture to also lecture treat. Audio: We are showing it by R and this R shows that this is like the error or the average error, whatever however we're measuring that error over the entire data set. And what we talked about last time was that this function is parametrized by TETA. What do I mean by parametrized by TETA? I mean that the SLR function that we had at the top is just the family of functions, right? It's just the template for all of the lines that we can have because depending on the TETA 0 and TETA 1 value, I can have different lines. And therefore, because this TETA value can change and because this prediction right here can change, Therefore, the residual can change and therefore, the value of my error or my model risk can also change. So that's why this is a function of PETA. And in this case, PETA is a vector that is including both PETA 0 and PETA 1 as well. text:
Audio: That's why everything is shown by just PETA, the annotation or the notation that we're using here just that it includes all the parameters that the model has. In this case, in simple linear regression, it's just two. There is TET-0 and TET-1. We also talked about at the end that we need to actually go ahead and estimate the optimum set of parameters. We did the calculus, we went ahead and calculated the partial derivative of the risk with respect to Teta 0 and also with respect to Teta 1. Two equations, two unknowns, we solved it and we arrived at the fact that Teta 0 hat and Teta 1 hat hat on top of the parameters means that that's the estimated parameter meaning that that's the optimum set of parameters that we can have is equal to exactly what you learned before in data aid, which is teta zero hat was equal to y bar minus, the slope times x bar and also the slope itself or teta one hat is equal to correlation times the standard division of y over standard division of x. Audio: That's the summary of last time. And we also talk briefly about visualization and evaluating and root-minus square error that we need to calculate. and I'm going to briefly talk about that in the next few slides. So kind of showing you what I meant by our function or the cost function being a parameter of theta. So what you're seeing here in two plots is that one plot is the optimum line with optimum set of parameters set to zero, hadn't had to one hat, and then the other one is a suboptimal example. In the suboptimal example, what you're seeing is that I have very large residuals. If you take a look at the residual, the mentally kind of draw all of those residual values and in your mind, the score, all of those residuals, so that it is going to penalize it even more. And average over all of those scores of the residuals, you can imagine that the suboptimal line is going to have a much higher risk as opposed to the optimal line that we have. And that's exactly what we're trying to do in order to find out the tetaside and tetaside to one value that is the optimum that passes through all of the lines as perfectly as possible and perfect means that we want to get the minimum scored error on average on all of the data points that we have. text:
Audio: Kind of reminding you of the terminology that I've been carefully using in the past lecture and also in today's lecture. There is a notion of estimation and there is a notion of production. When I talk about production, what do I mean by production? I mean that I have an input and I just want to plug into whatever model it is, regardless of whether it's optimal or non-optimal, and I want to get production. The production can be good, the production can be bad, So irrespective of that, any time that you're getting a prediction from any model, that's called a prediction task. When I go ahead and go through the process of finding the optimum set of parameters for the model using the data, so it's called using the data calculating model parameters. This is called estimation. So for example, when I have this simple linear regression, I would say that I go through the estimation process in order to find out that the zero hadn't, that the one had. And I call it estimation because we are basically estimating those parameters based on the data set that we have. Video: 1: lec11.ipynb - JupyterLab

Audio: So why is that the case? For example, say that I'm predicting some length value, length is the target that I'm predicting. If I tell you the error in, for example, meter is score, it's hard for you to make sense of what that meter is score is and how do you compare different models. As opposed to me going back to the meter kind of unit and telling you that, okay, the error on average is two meters, are you fine with it or not? And then you would with this type whether the model is accurate enough or not. It's just kind of a way of comparing different models and evaluating the performance of the model once we're done with the modeling piece. And then the last piece that I, again, mentioned was that we always want to visualize residuals. Remember, residual was calculated using this equation that we said that it's equal to the ground-trose value minus the predicted value. So there is sometimes that the residual is positive, sometimes that the residual is negative. audio: So why is that the case? For example, say that I'm predicting some length value, length is the target that I'm predicting. If I tell you the error in, for example, meter is score, it's hard for you to make sense of what that meter is score is and how do you compare different models. As opposed to me going back to the meter kind of unit and telling you that, okay, the error on average is two meters, are you fine with it or not? And then you would with this type whether the model is accurate enough or not. It's just kind of a way of comparing different models and evaluating the performance of the model once we're done with the modeling piece. And then the last piece that I, again, mentioned was that we always want to visualize residuals. Remember, residual was calculated using this equation that we said that it's equal to the ground-trose value minus the predicted value. So there is sometimes that the residual is positive, sometimes that the residual is negative. And what we learned from data aid is that if we plot the residual plot, we do not want to see any pattern.

Audio: The reason that we don't want to see any pattern is because if you see a pattern in the residual, that means that there was some pattern in the underlying data that you did the model. So your model was not capturing the entire trend or the entire characteristic in the data. So you have to go back. Do any kind of transformation that we'll see at the end of today's lecture in order to make sure that you literally model everything that's possible in the data so that the remaining residual is basically noise in measurement kind of. And that's why we want to make sure that the residual plot is kind of uniformly distributed across X and also across Y and there is no. kind of pattern that we can see in the output. So I'm going to start by talking about a specific dataset. I'm going to show you the demo. This is specific dataset is called Anzcom Cortet. There is four datasets in this specific dataset. And we're going to take a look at the characteristics of all of these four datasets. So with all of these four datasets that we have, The mean is standard, the average of x, average of y, a standard deviation for x and y, and the correlations are exactly the same, but the data sets are different. We will first define some helper functions to calculate the regression line for us.
[3]:
# Helper functions
def standard_units(x):
return (x - np.mean(x)) / np.std(x)
def correlation(x, y):
return np.mean(standard_units(x) * standard_units(y))
def slope(x, y):
return correlation(x, y) * np.std(y) / np.std(x)
def intercept(x, y):
return np.mean(y) - slope(x, y)*np.mean(x)

ahat, bhat = fit_least_squares(x, y)

# visualization
fig, ax_resid = None, None
if visualize == RESID_SCATTER:
fig, axs = plt.subplots(1,2, figsize=(8, 3))
axs[0].scatter(x, y)
axs[0].plot(x, yhat)
axs[0].set_title("LS fit")
ax_resid = axs [1]
elif visualize == RESID:
fig = plt.figure(figsize=(4, 3))
ax_resid = plt.gca()
if ax_resid is not None:
ax_resid.scatter(x, y - yhat, color = 'red' )
ax_resid.plot([4, 14], [0, 0], color = 'black')
ax_resid.set_title("Residuals")
return fig

# Helper functions
def standard_units(x):
return (x - np.mean(x)) / np.std(x)
def correlation(x, y):
return np.mean(standard_units(x) * standard_units(y))
def slope(x, y):
return correlation(x, y) * np.std(y) / np.std(x)
def intercept(x, y):
return np.mean(y) - slope(x, y)*np.mean(x)
def fit_least_squares(x, y):
theta_0 = intercept(x, y)
theta_1 = slope(x, y)
return theta_0, theta_1
def predict(x, theta_0, theta_1):
return theta_0 + theta_1*x
def compute_mse(y, yhat) :
return np.mean((y - yhat) ** 2)

# Performance metrics
ahat, bhat = fit_least_squares(x, y)
yhat = predict(x, ahat, bhat)
print(f"\theta_0: {ahat: . 2f}, \theta_1: {bhat :. 2f}")
print(f"RMSE: {np.sqrt(compute_mse(y, yhat)) :. 3f}")
# visualization
fig, ax_resid = None, None
if visualize == RESID_SCATTER:
fig, axs = plt.subplots(1,2, figsize=(8, 3))
axs[0].scatter(x, y)
axs[0].plot(x, yhat)
axs[0].set_title("LS fit")
ax_resid = axs [1]
elif visualize == RESID:
fig = plt.figure(figsize=(4, 3))
ax_resid = plt.gca()
if ax_resid is not None:
ax_resid.scatter(x, y - yhat, color = 'red' )
ax_resid.plot([4, 14], [0, 0], color = 'black')
ax_resid.set_title("Residuals")
return fig

We will first define some helper functions to calculate the regression line for us.
[3]:
# Helper functions
def standard_units(x):
return (x - np.mean(x)) / np.std(x)
def correlation(x, y):
return np.mean(standard_units(x) * standard_units(y))
def slope(x, y):
return correlation(x, y) * np.std(y) / np.std(x)
def intercept(x, y):
return np.mean(y) - slope(x, y)*np.mean(x)
def fit_least_squares(x, y):
theta_0 = intercept(x, y)
theta_1 = slope(x, y)
return theta_0, theta_1
def predict(x, theta_0, theta_1):
return theta_0 + theta_1*x
def compute_mse(y, yhat) :
return np.mean((y - yhat) ** 2)
[4] :

# statistics
print(f"x_mean : {np.mean(x) :. 2f}, y_mean : {np.mean(y) :. 2f}")
print(f"x stdev: {nn. std(x) : . 2f}. v stdev: {nn.std(v) : . 2f}")

# Helper functions
def standard_units(x):
return (x - np.mean(x)) / np.std(x)
def correlation(x, y) :
return np.mean(standard_units(x) * standard_units(y))
def slope(x, y):
return correlation(x, y) * np.std(y) / np.std(x)
def intercept(x, y):
return np.mean(y) - slope(x, y)*np.mean(x)
def fit_least_squares(x, y):
theta_0 = intercept(x, y)
theta_1 = slope(x, y)
return theta_0, theta_1
def predict (x, theta_0, theta_1):
return theta_0 + theta_1*x
def Jompute_mse(y, yhat):
return np.mean((y - yhat) ** 2)

# statistics
print(f"x_mean : {np.mean(x) :. 2f}, y_mean : {np.mean(y) :. 2f}")
print(f"x_stdev: {np.std(x) :. 2f}, y_stdev: {np.std(y) : . 2f}")
print(f"r = Correlation(x, y): {correlation(x, y) :. 3f}")
# Performance metrics
ahat, bhat = fit_least_squares(x, y)
yhat = predict(x, ahat, bhat)
print(f"\theta_0: {ahat: . 2f}, \theta_1: {bhat :. 2f}")
print(f"RMSE: {np.sqrt(compute_mse(y, yhat)) :. 3f}")
# visualization
fig, ax_resid = None, None
if visualize == RESID_SCATTER:
fig, axs = plt.subplots(1,2, figsize=(8, 3))
axs[0].scatter(x, y)
axs[0].plot(x, yhat)
axs[0].set_title("LS fit")
ax_resid = axs [1]
elif visualize == RESID:
fig = plt.figure(figsize=(4, 3))
ax_resid = plt.gca()
if ax_resid is not None:
ax_resid.scatter(x, y - yhat, color = 'red' )
ax_resid.plot([4, 14], [0, 0], color = 'black')
ax_resid.set_title("Residuals")
return fig

So what I'm going to do is I'm going to actually go ahead and try to plot these data sets so you can kind of compare that. So I'm going to zoom out slightly. I can zoom out, but this is data set one. So you can see that, okay, there was a correlation of point eight. So it's kind of a linear positive trend between X and Y. That's fine. For this one, the correlation says that is point one, but you're seeing that there is no linear trend, but it's a higher order relationship between x and y. But this one is a linear trend, but there is an outlying outlier sitting right here. And with this one, it's a line, but it's just the vertical line, and there is also an outlier, that's x4 or data set 4 that we had in the data set. So if I go ahead and just plot the line that we got, which basically had the same tets of zero and tets of 1 value for all of these four data sets, what we will get is actually all of these lines. text:
Audio: So you see there is this line for data set one, this line for data set two, for data set three and data set four. So this is kind of the case that I went ahead and calculated the statistics. Correlation was high enough, I went ahead and did the modeling. But I should have visualized the data set beforehand so that I could see visually that there is nonlinear trend or there is outlier that I have to remove before doing the modeling. And because I've not done that, then the residual plots are kind of looking funny. So for this case, the residual plot is fine. So this is the residual plot that we're plotting, which is equal to residual being equal to 0, which basically means that y minus y hat is equal to 0, which effectively means that y is equal to y hat. So that's the line of perfect prediction. So if the points are on that line, that's perfect prediction. If the points are above that line, that means that there was under estimation and then over estimation is under the line. So that's kind of how you can see. Because we are we know from the previous lecture that with msc the average errors or the average residuals is equal to equal to zero. Video: 1: lec11 - JupyterLab

audio: So you see there is this line for data set one, this line for data set two, for data set three and data set four. So this is kind of the case that I went ahead and calculated the statistics. Correlation was high enough, I went ahead and did the modeling. But I should have visualized the data set beforehand so that I could see visually that there is nonlinear trend or there is outlier that I have to remove before doing the modeling. And because I've not done that, then the residual plots are kind of looking funny. So for this case, the residual plot is fine. So this is the residual plot that we're plotting, which is equal to residual being equal to 0, which basically means that y minus y hat is equal to 0, which effectively means that y is equal to y hat. So that's the line of perfect prediction. So if the points are on that line, that's perfect prediction. If the points are above that line, that means that there was under estimation and then over estimation is under the line. So that's kind of how you can see. Because we are we know from the previous lecture that with msc the average errors or the average residuals is equal to equal to zero.

text:
Audio: Because I haven't told you what should be your objective function and I haven't told you what day tomorrow is right so you're missing that input information. But let alone if you're doing the constant model depending on how I want to penalize your estimation your objective function is going to be different right. And we're going to take a look at how different objective functions might lead to different outcomes or different optimum value that you can. So this was a constant model. And I tell you that how many bobbosies you will sell tomorrow. And I come back the day after and I tell how many bobbosies you will sell tomorrow and tomorrow. Effectively, you have to have a way of measuring how good your estimation should be and how to penalize your own miscalculations or mis-predictions. And you will keep telling me the same value. You will not change your information or you change your model because there is no input to your data. And you only have those five days. and you will keep giving me the same constant value. So a constant model is a model that is also called summary statistic. Video: 1: lec11 - JupyterLab

Audio: And again, you know that I've repeated it multiple times that is parametrized by TETA, depending on teta 0 value that you pick, you'll get a different loss value. And our goal is to find out what is the optimum teta 0 that gives us the minimum cost function for the cost value for this objective function. What I've done here is that I replaced yi had with teta 0 because that makes it simpler. So my function is now a very simple function that the y i's are always given from the data set and the only other argument that I have or the parameter that I have is theta 0. And I just need to optimize this function that is parametrized by theta 0, with respect to theta 0, which means that I can just use direct derivatives. So writing it again. There are different approaches that I can do it. There is one way that I can just go through calculus, just take the derivative, find the optimum point and the minimum point, and then report that minimum point as the optimum theta zero hat value. So we'll keep thinking about how I can... Take the derivative of that single term that is hidden between multiple summations and also it is a score as well. import itertools
import plotly. graph_objects as go
def mse_linear(theta_0, theta_1, data_linear):
data_x, data_y = data_linear. iloc[ : , 0], data_linear. iloc[ :, 1]
return np.mean(np.array([(y - (theta_0+theta_1*x))
** 2 for x, y in zip(data_x, data_y)]), axis=0)
theta_0_values = np. linspace(-80, 20, 80)
theta_1_values = np. linspace(-10,30, 80)
mse_values = [mse_linear(theta_0, theta_1, data_linear) \
for theta_0, theta_1 in itertools. product(theta_0_values, theta_1_values) ]
mse_values = np.reshape(mse_values, (len(theta_0_values), len(theta_1_values)), order='F')

# Just run this cell
import itertools
import plotly. graph_objects as go
def mse_linear(theta_0, theta_1, data_linear):
data_x, data_y = data_linear. iloc[ : , 0], data_linear. iloc[ :, 1]
return np.mean(np.array([(y - (theta_0+theta_1*x))
** 2 for x, y in zip(data_x, data_y)]), axis=0)
theta_0_values = np. linspace(-80, 20, 80)
theta_1_values = np. linspace(-10,30, 80)
mse_values = [mse_linear(theta_0, theta_1, data_linear) \
for theta_0, theta_1 in itertools. product(theta_0_values, theta_1_values) ]
mse_values = np.reshape(mse_values, (len(theta_0_values), len(theta_1_values)), order='F')

import itertools
import plotly. graph_objects as go
def mse_linear(theta_0, theta_1, data_linear):
data_x, data_y = data_linear. iloc[ : , 0], data_linear. iloc[ :, 1]
return np.mean(np.array([(y - (theta_0+theta_1*x))
** 2 for x, y in zip(data_x, data_y)]), axis=0)
theta_0_values = np. linspace(-80, 20, 80)
theta_1_values = np. linspace(-10,30, 80)
mse_values = [mse_linear(theta_0, theta_1, data_linear) \
for theta_0, theta_1 in itertools. product(theta_0_values, theta_1_values) ]
mse_values = np.reshape(mse_values, (len(theta_0_values), len(theta_1_values)), order='F')

import itertools
import plotly. graph_objects as go
def mse_linear(theta_0, theta_1, data_linear):
data_x, data_y = data_linear. iloc[ : , 0], data_linear. iloc[ : , 1]
return np.mean(np.array([(y - (theta_0+theta_1*x))
** 2 for x, y in zip(data_x, data_y)]), axis=0)
theta_0_values = np. linspace(-80, 20, 80)
theta_1_values = np. linspace(-10,30, 80)
mse_values = [mse_linear(theta_0, theta_1, data_linear) \
for theta_0, theta_1 in itertools. product(theta_0_values, theta_1_values) ]
mse_values = np.reshape(mse_values, (len(theta_0_values), len(theta_1_values)), order='F')

# Just run this cell
import itertools
import plotly. graph_objects as go
def mse_linear(theta_0, theta_1, data_linear):
data_x, data_y = data_linear. iloc[ : , 0], data_linear.iloc[ :, 1]
return np.mean(np.array([(y - (theta_0+theta_1*x))
** 2 for x, y in zip(data_x, data_y)]), axis=0)
theta_0_values = np.linspace(-80, 20, 80)
theta_1_values = np.linspace(-10,30, 80)
mse_values = [mse_linear(theta_0, theta_1, data_linear) \
for theta_0, theta_1 in itertools. product(theta_0_values, theta_1_values) ]
mse_values = np.reshape(mse_values, (len(theta_0_values), len(theta_1_values)), order='F' )
# Optimal point
data_x, data_y = data_linear. iloc[ : , 0];
data_linear.iloc[ :, 1]

import itertools
import plotly.graph_objects as go
def mse_linear(theta_0, theta_1, data_linear):
data_x, data_y = data_linear.iloc[ :, 0], data_linear.iloc[ :, 1]
return np.mean(np.array([(y - (theta_0+theta_1*x)) ** 2 for x, y in zip(data_x, data_y)]), axis=0)

theta_1_values = np. linspace(-10,30, 80)
mse_values = [mse_linear(theta_0, theta_1, data_linear) \
for theta_0, theta_1 in itertools. product(theta_0_values, theta_1_values) ]
mse_values = np.reshape(mse_values, (len(theta_0_values), len(theta_1_values)), order='F')
# Optimal point
data_x, data_y = data_linear.iloc[ :, 0], data_linear.iloc[ : , 1]
theta_1_hat = np. corrcoef(data_x, data_y) [0, 1] * np.std(data_y) / np.std(data_x)
theta_0_hat = np.mean(data_y) - theta_1_hat * np.mean(data_x)
fig_lin = go. Figure(data=[go. Surface(x=theta_0_values, y=theta_1_values, z=mse_values, name='z=MSE' )])
fig_lin.add_trace(go. Scatter3d(x=[theta_0_hat], y=[theta_1_hat], \

fig_lin.update_layout(
autosize=False,

text:
Audio: You see that when there is dark blue, that's when we have a low low cost. And when it's yellow, that's when we have a very large cost. So what we want to do is we want to be really down in the valley and we want to find out what is the optimum value that gives us that minimum minimum point. It happens that if I rotate this, you will see the shape. This is literally a 2 3D Surface a three-dimensional space. Right? And if I take a look at where I have the optimum, you see that my optimum is actually happening in this orange piece. This orange piece is telling me that, okay, what are those attributes of the tetahed, it says that tetas 0 is equal to negative 44, tetah 1 is equal to 23, and then z is equal to 18, which means that the cost is equal to 18. But do I care about 18? Probably no, I care about that zero hadn't had to one half. It's interesting when you compare that 18 to this value right up here, which you see that the cost is equal to 12,000. Video: 1: lec11 - JupyterLab

audio: You see that when there is dark blue, that's when we have a low low cost. And when it's yellow, that's when we have a very large cost. So what we want to do is we want to be really down in the valley and we want to find out what is the optimum value that gives us that minimum minimum point. It happens that if I rotate this, you will see the shape. This is literally a 2 3D Surface a three-dimensional space. Right? And if I take a look at where I have the optimum, you see that my optimum is actually happening in this orange piece. This orange piece is telling me that, okay, what are those attributes of the tetahed, it says that tetas 0 is equal to negative 44, tetah 1 is equal to 23, and then z is equal to 18, which means that the cost is equal to 18. But do I care about 18? Probably no, I care about that zero hadn't had to one half. It's interesting when you compare that 18 to this value right up here, which you see that the cost is equal to 12,000.

Audio: Olivia is the winner of the questions of today. Olivia is asking, do we know if a function is convex or not? When we're always assuming that, oh, you take the derivative of the set of equal to zero and you always say that that's minimum. and... That's a valid concern. We always need to check for the second derivative to make sure that the function is actually convex, meaning that the optimum point is always the minimum point. And I'm going to take a look at that together with you in a second in the next few slides. Okay. So the next model is going to be an easy model now that you've gone through doing constant model with msc doing constant model with ma is going to be easier. What was the MAE? MAE was mean absolute error. Remember that we had the last function, or two last, L1 last, this is L1 last, but taking average over all of the data points that we have, which we call it mean absolute error. So I'm going to take a look at how I can fit a model and how that looks like. Video: 1: V

text:
Audio: So what we would basically take a look at is that, okay, for this is specific data point, for different choices of teta 0 value, again, notice that teta 0 is on the x-axis. I have different loss values and it depends on whether it's 0 is less than 20 or above 20 because I have that absolute value sign so sometimes this slope is negative, sometimes this slope is positive. So that's basically the shape of this loss function for a single data point. When I want to consider all the data points that I have right here and I take the average over all of those absolute value of functions. Effectively, I'll get this M-A-E function, that the M-A-E function has one of those like v-shaped functions here for 20, one of those v-shaped functions for 21, for 22, 29, and 33. And then if I sum them up together and calculate the average, what I'll get is this kind of a He survives linear function that has multiple kinks and the location that it has kinks is that the point that we have each one of these individual data points in our data set right so we'd see that there is one at 20 there is one at 21 there is one at 22 and then 29 and then 33 are all of those like breaking points that we have. I have different loss values and it depends on whether it's 0 is less than 20 or above 20 because I have that absolute value sign so sometimes this slope is negative, sometimes this slope is positive. So that's basically the shape of this loss function for a single data point. When I want to consider all the data points that I have right here and I take the average over all of those absolute value of functions. Effectively, I'll get this M-A-E function, that the M-A-E function has one of those like v-shaped functions here for 20, one of those v-shaped functions for 21, for 22, 29, and 33. And then if I sum them up together and calculate the average, what I'll get is this kind of a He survives linear function that has multiple kinks and the location that it has kinks is that the point that we have each one of these individual data points in our data set right so we'd see that there is one at 20 there is one at 21 there is one at 22 and then 29 and then 33 are all of those like breaking points that we have.

And then what's the impact of having different loss functions in order to what final value of optimum parameters we're going to arrive at? So every time we calculated the derivative or partial derivative, we set it equal to zero, we solved it. We found that those optimum parameters. One thing that I didn't tell you, and I briefly told you is that the function needs to be convex for the first derivative to be equal to the minimum. Video: 1: lec11 - JupyterLab

text:
Audio: And then you saw that with the M A E, we had this other function that was piecewise linear and the optimum was at 22. So that was kind of the difference between when we were doing the MSE versus MAE, and we arrived at different tetasero hat values. One time the tetasero hat was mean, one time the tetasero hat was equal to median. The other considerations was that one function was smooth, it was differentiable, this other function was not smooth. And then it was piecewise linear. And we're going to use some of these techniques later on in order to see how we can deal with all of these piecewise linear non-differentiable functions that we're going to have in different machine learning models. One more consideration. Remember that we had a bunch of two data sets in an SCOM quartet data set that had outlier and then it was just kind of messing up with the entire modeling piece. We want to take a look at the impact of having an outlier and the choice of the loss function. Video: 1: lec11 - JupyterLab

text:
Audio: So for example, in this case, you're seeing that I added a very large outlier data set, one thousand thirty trees, somehow you still have many properties in a day. And we're going to take a look at what that means to the Lost Landscape. Take a look at the Lost Landscape for MSC versus MAE. Remember that I was telling you that MSC is a function or the L2 loss is a function that penalizes larger miscalculations or mis-predictions more. So that's what's happening in this case. It's trying to really shift the model such that it's kind of a striking balance between predicting those smaller values that are close to each other and also having a little bit of mis-prediction for this large value so that it doesn't get a very huge escort loss for that outlier value. So that's why the tet is zero had in this case is equal to 293 as opposed to with the MAE loss is going to be more reliable. It's going to just slightly shift it again because it's just a median that's being shifted. Video: 1: lec11 - JupyterLab

text:
Audio: You're expecting that the outliers are going to be on two ends. So it's just generally going to shift your optimum point as likely to the left or to the right, but not substantially to turn it into 190. So what I would say is that MSC is more sensitive to outliers, but MAE is more robust. So that's the difference. That's the choice. If you're thinking of your data, it has a lot of outliers. One thing is you remove outliers. The second thing is that use a A last function that is less sensitive so that your predictions are not going to be so off. The other thing is the uniqueness of the solution. Say if I add another sample to your dataset, previously you had five days, now I'm going to give you the sixth day. And I'm going to tell you that, okay, go ahead and calculate your optimum tetasero hat for those cases. What you're seeing here is that But the M&C I still have the unique solution, the unique solution is just the average of whatever data points that I have. Video: 1: lec11 - JupyterLab

text:
Audio: Hopefully by now, you're a little bit more comfortable with transformation. Remember what we had in transformation, which was that I had this 2K muscle or bulge diagram, or we had a way of thinking about how to transform different axes so that the data is linearized. So the transform version of x with respect to the transform version of y is showing a linear relationship, which means that at that point you can use a regression line or a regression modeling for your modeling purposes. So in the case of this new Gong example, I'll tell you I said that, okay, I'm doing a simple linear regression and I'm happy with the simple linear regression compared to the constant model. If you go ahead and take a look at the residual plot, the residual plot kind of cares upward. So, I'll tell you that this is better than the constant model. It's still not perfect. It still shows pattern. I still need to transfer my data to make it my data ready for the proper modeling. Video: 1: v

text:
Audio: And I'm going to call log of y as a variable z for ease of use. And I would say that, okay, now I'm modeling z with respect to x. which Z is actually now going to be the log transform version of y which hopefully is going to linearize my data. So going back and taking a look at how it looks like. This is the original data, original line, original residual plot. If I go ahead and linearize it, you would see that now I have log of Z, log of h or log of y, or I'm calling it as Z. Which is which is showing a linear pattern with respect to the input so now the line passes through the data points a little bit more accurately and if I go ahead and take a look at the residual plot not the residual plot doesn't show any pattern. Which technically means that log of why is the better transformation to use with respect to the input x that I had and now linear regression model is going to be more appropriate otherwise if your residual plot shows the pattern that means that there is some pattern in the data that you you haven't captured so you have not done good enough of a job. Audio: So what does this mean? This means that okay, that was my data set, the data set of lengths with respect to age. I was not happy with just using this because I was seeing that the data set kind of cares upward. I did the transformation, I took the log of the age, so now I have log of the age, which I'm calling it as being equal to Z. And now the data is linear, I fit a linear regression line. So I have links with respect to log of age that I'm predicting, which again, this is the line and the equation for the line is going to be, for example, something as peta, I'm going to write it at the top. It's going to be equal to teta zero plus teta one times x, which x in this case is going to be my length variable. So what I can do next is that in order to go back to the original kind of scale of the data that I know x with respect to y, what I can do is I can Calculate e to the power of each side. Audio: And I want to go ahead and Find a model or a set of set of parameters that's going to give me my predictions. And I'm going to go after finding the estimated parameters that are going to be the best case scenarios with the minimum objective function or minimum loss across the entire data set that matches my predictions well to the ground truth values. So what we will do actually is move away from only living in one feature space or one dimensional feature space or taking only one input. And we're going to move on to the case that we have multiple different inputs. So you might have length, you might have, for example, weight, you might have different attributes for an instance or an object in order to make a prediction. So in that case, what we would say is that our model is going to be a multiple regression line. Multiple inputs are going to be combined together. It's a still a linear regression line. And then we basically calculate a weighted average of all of those inputs in order to make a prediction. Video: 1: Clec11 - JupyterLab

And what it will do is it will calculate evaded combination of all of them together. It basically says that you have teta 0. It is going to multiply feature 1 with the parameter that's associated with feature 1, which is teta 1, and then also the same thing with multiplying teta 2 we'll get to 2 or the vape for Feature 2 is going to be get to 2 multiplied with Feature 2, so on and so forth, up until the point that you get this final production. So the example could be that if you take a look at my cat and you want to basically take a look at different data points of my cat over multiple years and I have multiple different features for her.
